narrative: As a Black male applicant with a lower-middle family income background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.10—below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 23.50, which is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.70. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 97% probability that you would fail the bar exam, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure risk means the model saw your combination of factors as strongly signaling challenges in passing the bar on the first try, a key benchmark for law school admissions. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, far below the average decile of about 5.5. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which significantly pulled the prediction toward failure because it was so much lower than what the model associates with bar success. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 1 also contributed toward the failure outlook, reinforcing a pattern of lower early academic performance compared to typical students who rank around the middle of their classes. While your full-time enrollment plans align with the vast majority of students (over 92%), they didn't offset these core academic concerns. Overall, your situation compares to typical admitted students by showing weaker performance metrics across key early indicators, where stronger rankings and test scores usually tip predictions toward passing the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many factors like family income play a role in opportunities—but the model prioritizes these bar passage predictors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and targeting schools with strong support for first-generation or underrepresented students like yourself. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also help in reapplying, perhaps after gaining relevant experience. With targeted improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
